Also, the concept might have roots in Shinto, which has a lot to do with nature and the sacredness of the present moment. But I'm not sure how exactly these traditions influence wabi-sabi.

Then the key elements. Wabi-sabi is about appreciating the transient and imperfect. So things like cracks in pottery, weathered wood, or faded colors. It's not about making things perfect but finding beauty in their imperfections. There's also a sense of humility and acceptance of the natural cycle of life. Maybe it's about living in the moment and not being overly attached to material things.
